explanation: This LTL property captures the requirement that the autopilot will eventually transition to the STANDBY state from the TRANSITION state when the pilot is in control (standby).  The use of '->' implies that when standby is true, the next state will be standby. G(standby -> F state_is_STANDBY) would not fully capture this because if the pilot is in standby for an extended time, the autopilot could stay in TRANSITION indefinitely and this LTL would be satisfied. The use of the until operator U ensures that the transition to STANDBY must occur while standby is true.

This LTL property captures the requirement that the autopilot transitions from the TRANSITION state to the STANDBY state when the pilot takes control.  The property uses the atomic proposition standby to indicate that the pilot is in control and the atomic propositions state_is_TRANSITION and state_is_STANDBY to represent the autopilot's states. The implication ensures that if the pilot is in control (standby is true), then eventually the autopilot will be in the STANDBY state (state_is_STANDBY is true) after having been in the TRANSITION state (state_is_TRANSITION is true).

This LTL property captures the requirement that the autopilot will eventually transition to the STANDBY state when the pilot is in control.  The property uses the atomic proposition "standby" to represent the pilot being in control and "state_is_STANDBY" to represent the autopilot being in the STANDBY state. The implication ensures that whenever standby is true, eventually the autopilot will also be in the STANDBY state. The use of F (eventually) accounts for potential delays in the state transition. It does not specify that a transition from TRANSITION must occur.

This LTL property captures the requirement that whenever the autopilot is in the TRANSITION state and the pilot is in control (standby), the next state will be STANDBY.  It uses the atomic propositions to represent the states and the pilot's control status. The use of X operator ensures the state transition happens in the next step.

This LTL property captures the requirement that the autopilot will eventually transition to the STANDBY state when the pilot is in control (standby).  The property uses the atomic proposition standby to represent the pilot being in control and state_is_STANDBY to represent the autopilot being in the STANDBY state. The implication ensures that if the pilot is in control, the autopilot will eventually transition to STANDBY.

This LTL property captures the requirement that the autopilot transitions from the TRANSITION state to the STANDBY state when the pilot is in control (standby).  The property states that if the autopilot is in the TRANSITION state (state_is_TRANSITION), then it will eventually reach the STANDBY state (state_is_STANDBY) when the pilot is in control (standby). This is expressed by using the "until" (U) operator. The formula ensures that as long as the system is in the TRANSITION state, it will eventually reach the STANDBY state once the condition standby becomes true.

This LTL property captures the requirement that the autopilot will eventually transition to the STANDBY state when the pilot is in control.  The property uses the atomic proposition "standby" to represent the pilot being in control and the atomic propositions "state_is_TRANSITION" and "state_is_STANDBY" to represent the autopilot's state. The implication (->) ensures that if the standby condition is true, then the autopilot will eventually transition to the STANDBY state. It does not specify a direct transition in the next state.

This LTL property captures the requirement that whenever the autopilot is in the TRANSITION state and the pilot is in control (standby), the next state will be STANDBY.  It uses the atomic propositions state_is_TRANSITION and standby to represent the conditions, and the next operator (X) to indicate the transition to the next state. The state_is_STANDBY atomic proposition represents the STANDBY state.

It uses the atomic proposition "state_is_TRANSITION" to detect when the system is in the TRANSITION state and "state_is_STANDBY" to detect when the system is in the STANDBY state. The atomic proposition "standby" signifies that the pilot is in control. The property ensures that the transition from TRANSITION to STANDBY will happen eventually once standby is true and the system is in the TRANSITION state.

The implication ensures that if the pilot is in control, eventually the autopilot will be in STANDBY state.

This LTL property states that it is always the case that if the autopilot is in the TRANSITION state, then it will eventually transition to the STANDBY state when the pilot is in control (standby).  The use of implies ensures that the transition only occurs when the condition is met, and the use of F guarantees the transition happens eventually, making it more robust to transient conditions than other temporal operators like U or V. This property also correctly reflects the natural language, which indicates a causal relationship rather than simply a concurrent relationship.

This LTL property states that, globally, if the autopilot is in the TRANSITION state and the pilot is in control (standby), then in the next state the autopilot will be in STANDBY.  It directly captures the meaning of the input natural language.

It uses the atomic propositions to represent the states of the autopilot and the pilot's control status. The operator '->' represents implication, ensuring that the transition to STANDBY happens if the condition of the pilot being in control is met. The 'U' operator is used to indicate that the transition should happen until the STANDBY state is reached.

This LTL property captures the requirement that when the pilot is in control (standby is true), the autopilot will eventually transition from the TRANSITION state to the STANDBY state.  It uses the 'until' operator (U) to express the condition that the autopilot will remain in the TRANSITION state until the STANDBY state is reached, at which point the transition is considered complete. The use of G ensures that this behavior will always hold, which corresponds to "shall" in the input natural language.
